category_title: Students who express vocal and physical resistance to perceived injustice or rigid schedules
scenario_title: Reacting to discriminatory historical content during a read-aloud
minimal_context: The class is participating in a read-aloud of the book 'Separate is Never Equal' and looking at an illustration of a sign at a public pool.

dialogue.speaker: tutor
dialogue.message: Let's look at the illustration on this page. The sign here says, 'No dogs or Mexicans allowed: Public pool.'

dialogue.speaker: student
dialogue.message: (Lifts his hands in the air) The white people. I think, think… the white people were saying they were Mexicans but they were not.

dialogue.speaker: student
dialogue.message: I think… I think the white people should go on the other side of the earth!

dialogue.speaker: tutor
dialogue.message: Let's just focus on what the book says next.

dialogue.speaker: student
dialogue.message: (Calling out) Well… well… It’s just that the white people need to go over there until they learn their lesson and then they can come back. How they are is not okay. They are being unkind to the Brown people!

dialogue_combined:
TUTOR: Let's look at the illustration on this page. The sign here says, 'No dogs or Mexicans allowed: Public pool.'

STUDENT: (Lifts his hands in the air) The white people. I think, think… the white people were saying they were Mexicans but they were not.

TUTOR: What makes you say that?

STUDENT: I think… I think the white people should go on the other side of the earth!

TUTOR: Let's just focus on what the book says next.

STUDENT: (Calling out) Well… well… It’s just that the white people need to go over there until they learn their lesson and then they can come back. How they are is not okay. They are being unkind to the Brown people!
